diary: Grey Tuesday, drizzly like the city forgot to turn the brightness back on. I woke up in Newcastle, made coffee, and did that quick pre-day tidy that always makes me feel like I’ve got control over something. Around Newcastle Beach Park I kept my little scenic loop short—rain was already starting to win, and I didn’t want to arrive soaked and cranky. The drive to Redmond felt extra careful, buffer-heavy, like I could will the traffic to behave. At Microsoft Building 1, the morning product sync went smoother than expected; the metrics were steady even if my brain wasn’t. I ducked into Starbucks for warmth and a quick bite, then knuckled down on the PRD—user feedback can be so specific it’s almost kind. With SR-520 closed eastbound, I stayed local for errands and lunch, then knitted at home while the rain turned gentler. Later, tea and conversation at Bellevue Downtown Park felt like a reset button. I’m tired, but in a good, grounded way.
thought_process: I planned my day around the rain and the SR-520 closure without making it dramatic. The extra commute buffer was less about fear and more about preserving focus—if I arrive stressed, I carry it into product decisions. I chose warmer indoor stops (Starbucks, then lunch) to keep momentum and avoid getting swallowed by the weather. At work, I leaned into the slower window after standup to clarify roadmap assumptions; it felt like the best use of attention before the afternoon working session. World events weren’t “in the headlines” for me today, but the bridge closure absolutely shaped my routes and timing, and my mood followed: a little frazzled in the morning, then calmer once I had a clear plan and steady progress. Knitting and a low-key catch-up helped me end softer than I started.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
